question: A 70-year-old man underwent repeat endovascular transvenous coil packing of a dural venous sinus via a torcular approach. Postprocedural contrast-enhanced MR venography was obtained (image shown). Which procedural complication best explains the postprocedural imaging appearance?

options.A: Acute thrombosis of the left transverse sinus
options.B: Coil migration into the torcular confluence
options.D: Iatrogenic epidural hematoma compressing the sinus

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image: a contrast-enhanced 3D MR venogram volume rendering with the right and left transverse sinuses labeled. The right transverse sinus is smoothly opacified and continuous, whereas the left transverse sinus shows an abrupt loss of normal opacification with a tangled, nonfilling segment at the torcular region (area indicated by the arrowhead). A narrow channel from the right toward the torcular is visible (arrow), but distal filling of the left transverse sinus is markedly reduced/absent.

Next, I relate these findings to the question, which asks which procedural complication best explains this postprocedural appearance after transvenous coil packing via a torcular approach. The key image feature is loss of contrast opacification/abrupt cutoff of the left transverse sinus consistent with an occlusion of that venous segment.

Then I consider each option against the imaging: acute thrombosis of the left transverse sinus (A) would produce the observed absent opacification/abrupt cutoff — this matches the image. Coil migration into the torcular (B) would be expected to show a focal high-density coil mass occupying the confluence region with corresponding artifact or distortion, which is not seen here. A persistent arteriovenous shunt (C) would more likely show abnormal early arterialized filling or enlarged draining veins, not an absent venous lumen. An epidural hematoma compressing the sinus (D) would produce external mass effect or displacement visible on cross-sectional imaging; there is no evidence of an extra-axial compressive collection on this venogram.

Therefore, the imaging appearance is best explained by acute thrombosis/occlusion of the left transverse sinus.
caption: A case of approach into the isolated sinus with the SM. (A) Frontal view of an angiogram of the left external carotid arteriography shows Borden type 3 TSDAVF. (B) 3D volume rendering (superior view) of the gadolinium-enhanced MR image shows the narrow access route from rt. TS to lt. TS (white arrow) and occlusion of the lt. TS due to thrombosis (white arrowhead). (C and D) These images show the tip movement of the SM (black arrows). (E) Intraoperative image shows that a 1.9F microcatheter is navigated to the isolated sinus. (F) Posttransvenous coil embolization. (G) Postprocedural left external carotid arteriography shows complete occlusion of TSDAVF. lt.: left; rt.: right; SM: steerable microcatheter; TS: transverse sinus; TSDAVF: transverse–sigmoid sinus dural arteriovenous fistula
sub_caption: (B) 3D volume rendering (superior view) of the gadolinium-enhanced MR image shows the narrow access route from rt. TS to lt. TS (white arrow) and occlusion of the lt. TS due to thrombosis (white arrowhead).
image_context: Case 2 was a 70-year-old man who presented with right pulsatile tinnitus. The patient had undergone endovascular treatment for a Borden type 3 left TSSDAVF 6 years earlier, and a repeat treatment was planned for a recurrent lesion. Cerebral angiography revealed Borden type 3 left TSSDAVF with isolated sinus and cortical venous reflux (<xref rid="jnet-17-153-g003" ref-type="fig"><bold>Fig. 3A</bold></xref>). In the first procedure, coil packing the left transverse sinus had been performed. Therefore, the present treatment required an approach to the isolated sinus via torcular Herophili for sTVE. Under general anesthesia, a 5F guiding sheath (Axcelguide) was introduced from bilateral femoral veins into right internal jugular vein. A 4.2F distal access catheter (Fubuki) and the triple coaxial system of a 1.9F microcatheter (Carnelian MARVEL) through the high-flow-type SM were inserted into the 5F guiding sheath of the right femoral vein. A 3.2F distal access catheter (TACTICS; Technocrat, Aichi, Japan) and a coaxial system of a 1.7F microcatheter (SL-10; Stryker, Kalamazoo, MI, USA) were inserted into the 5F guiding sheath of the right femoral vein. First, a 1.7F microcatheter was used to cannulate the isolated sinus via the sinus confluence, although it was difficult due to stenosis of access route. However, by adjusting the angle of the tip of the steering catheter, catheterization into the isolated sinus was achieved (Fig. 3A). In the first procedure, coil packing the left transverse sinus had been performed. Therefore, the present treatment required an approach to the isolated sinus via torcular Herophili for sTVE. Under general anesthesia, a 5F guiding sheath (Axcelguide) was introduced from bilateral femoral veins into right internal jugular vein. A 4.2F distal access catheter (Fubuki) and the triple coaxial system of a 1.9F microcatheter (Carnelian MARVEL) through the high-flow-type SM were inserted into the 5F guiding sheath of the right femoral vein. A 3.2F distal access catheter (TACTICS; Technocrat, Aichi, Japan) and a coaxial system of a 1.7F microcatheter (SL-10; Stryker, Kalamazoo, MI, USA) were inserted into the 5F guiding sheath of the right femoral vein. First, a 1.7F microcatheter was used to cannulate the isolated sinus via the sinus confluence, although it was difficult due to stenosis of access route. However, by adjusting the angle of the tip of the steering catheter, catheterization into the isolated sinus was achieved (<xref rid="jnet-17-153-g003" ref-type="fig"><bold>Fig. 3B</bold></xref> and Fig. 3B and <xref rid="jnet-17-153-g003" ref-type="fig"><bold>3C</bold></xref>). Subsequently, directly packing the isolated sinus using the turn-back embolization technique was performed (3C). Subsequently, directly packing the isolated sinus using the turn-back embolization technique was performed (<xref rid="jnet-17-153-g003" ref-type="fig"><bold>Fig. 3D</bold></xref> and Fig. 3D and <xref rid="jnet-17-153-g003" ref-type="fig"><bold>3E</bold></xref>). Finally, complete occlusion of the TSSDAVF was achieved (3E). Finally, complete occlusion of the TSSDAVF was achieved (<xref rid="jnet-17-153-g003" ref-type="fig"><bold>Fig. 3F</bold></xref>).Fig. 3F).
relevant_image_context: Case 2 was a 70-year-old man who presented with right pulsatile tinnitus. The patient had undergone endovascular treatment for a Borden type 3 left TSSDAVF 6 years earlier, and a repeat treatment was planned for a recurrent lesion. Cerebral angiography revealed Borden type 3 left TSSDAVF with isolated sinus and cortical venous reflux. In the first procedure, coil packing the left transverse sinus had been performed. Therefore, the present treatment required an approach to the isolated sinus via torcular Herophili for sTVE. Under general anesthesia, a 5F guiding sheath (Axcelguide) was introduced from bilateral femoral veins into right internal jugular vein. A 4.2F distal access catheter (Fubuki) and the triple coaxial system of a 1.9F microcatheter (Carnelian MARVEL) through the high-flow-type SM were inserted into the 5F guiding sheath of the right femoral vein. A 3.2F distal access catheter (TACTICS; Technocrat, Aichi, Japan) and a coaxial system of a 1.7F microcatheter (SL-10; Stryker, Kalamazoo, MI, USA) were inserted into the 5F guiding sheath of the right femoral vein. First, a 1.7F microcatheter was used to cannulate the isolated sinus via the sinus confluence, although it was difficult due to stenosis of access route. However, by adjusting the angle of the tip of the steering catheter, catheterization into the isolated sinus was achieved. Subsequently, directly packing the isolated sinus using the turn-back embolization technique was performed. Finally, complete occlusion of the TSSDAVF was achieved.

knowledge.topic: venous sinus occlusion signs on venography
knowledge.claim_text: Absent contrast opacification or an abrupt cutoff of a venous sinus on venography typically suggests occlusion of that venous segment, such as acute thrombosis.
knowledge.presence_question: Does the model's response discuss venous sinus occlusion signs on venography?
knowledge.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly state that Absent contrast opacification or an abrupt cutoff of a venous sinus on venography typically suggests occlusion of that venous segment, such as acute thrombosis?

reasoning.claim_text: The abrupt loss of contrast opacification and the tangled nonfilling segment of the left transverse sinus at the torcular supports that the left transverse sinus is occluded, such as by acute thrombosis.
reasoning.presence_question: Does the model's response make an inferential link about left transverse sinus occlusion?
reasoning.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly conclude that The abrupt loss of contrast opacification and the tangled nonfilling segment of the left transverse sinus at the torcular supports that the left transverse sinus is occluded, such as by acute thrombosis.?

reasoning.claim_text: Smooth opacification and continuity of the right transverse sinus on the venogram supports that the right transverse sinus is patent.

reasoning.claim_text: The presence of a narrow channel from the right toward the torcular together with markedly reduced or absent distal filling of the left transverse sinus implies that the limited cross-filling does not reconstitute the distal left transverse sinus.
